questions.specification: Explain how the following factors can affect the sustainability of capture fisheries:

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.3F Conservation and Management of Global Fisheries (HL)

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.3C Maximum sustainable yield

parts.content: Overfishing
parts.markscheme:
### Definition
- Overfishing refers to the depletion of fish stocks beyond sustainable levels, where the rate of removal exceeds the rate of replenishment. <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Consequences

1. Reduced fish populations

Overfishing can drive fish populations below the level at which they can replenish themselves through reproduction. This leads to a decline in the overall fish stock.
<marks>1 mark</marks>

2. Ecosystem imbalances and trophic cascades

Overfishing can disrupt the delicate balance of marine ecosystems, leading to trophic cascades, where the removal of a key species causes imbalances throughout the food web.
<marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Other potential consequences could include loss of biodiversity, economic impacts on fishing communities, and disruption of nutrient cycles.</note>

parts.content: Bycatch
parts.markscheme:
- Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each point, up to <marks>2 marks</marks>:
  - Unintentional capture of non-target species
  - Can impact endangered species and overall biodiversity

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 4: Water > 4.3 Aquatic food production systems > 4.3F Conservation and Management of Global Fisheries (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.3F Conservation and Management of Global Fisheries (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question specifically asks for technological methods to improve the sustainability of capture fisheries, which falls directly under the conservation and management strategies for global fisheries discussed in this topic.

parts.content: Outline four ways in which technology is being used to improve the sustainability of capture fisheries.
parts.markscheme:
- Vessel monitoring systems (VMS) or AIS to track fishing effort/locations and support enforcement against illegal, unreported and unregulated (IUU) fishing <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Electronic monitoring (on-board cameras/e-logbooks) to improve reporting compliance and catch verification <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Selective gear technology (e.g., turtle excluder devices, bycatch reduction devices, circle hooks, LED deterrents) to reduce bycatch and discards <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Acoustic/sonar surveys and/or eDNA and/or remote sensing to improve fish-stock assessment and guide adaptive quotas/closures <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Real-time spatial management using satellite/oceanographic data to avoid sensitive habitats or spawning aggregations (dynamic closures) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Blockchain/QR traceability systems to improve chain-of-custody and reduce fraud/illegal product entering markets <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how the use of technology can affect the sustainability of capture fisheries.
parts.markscheme:
- Technology can increase sustainability by enabling better monitoring, control and surveillance (e.g., VMS/AIS/e-monitoring) so IUU fishing is reduced and rules are enforced <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Improved data collection (e-logbooks, observer apps, acoustic surveys, eDNA) increases accuracy of stock assessments and reduces uncertainty in setting TACs/quotas <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Selective gear innovations reduce bycatch, mortality of non-target species, and habitat damage (e.g., less benthic contact), supporting biodiversity <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Traceability tech (e.g., blockchain) can shift consumer/retailer demand toward certified/legal seafood, providing economic incentives for sustainable practices <marks>1 mark</marks>
- However, technology can also increase fishing efficiency (fish-finding sonar, FADs, GPS) leading to higher catchability and risk of overexploitation if governance is weak <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Technology costs may exclude small-scale fishers or shift effort to those who can invest, potentially creating equity issues and undermining compliance in some contexts <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Supported conclusion/value judgement: overall sustainability improves when technological advances are paired with effective management (precautionary quotas, enforcement capacity and transparent data), but may worsen sustainability if they mainly increase fishing power without control <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss the extent to which technological solutions alone can make global capture fisheries sustainable.
parts.markscheme:
- 0 marks: Not relevant or no standard reached.
- 1–3 marks: Limited understanding of sustainability in capture fisheries; simplistic statements about “more technology” improving fishing; little or no balance; few or no examples; conclusion absent or unsupported.
- 4–6 marks: Some accurate explanation of how technology can both improve monitoring/selectivity and increase fishing power; some balance and relevant examples (e.g., VMS, selective gear, fish-finding sonar); some linkage to management/governance; a conclusion is present but may be limited in support.
- 7–9 marks: Well-balanced, well-structured discussion of benefits (monitoring/enforcement, better stock assessment, bycatch reduction, traceability) and limitations (effort creep, rebound effects, unequal access, data manipulation, governance gaps); uses clear ESS terminology (MSY, precautionary approach, IUU fishing, bycatch, compliance) and well-developed examples; integrates broader constraints beyond technology (effective regulation, international cooperation, market incentives, ecosystem-based management, socio-economic factors); explicit, supported conclusion on the extent to which technology alone is sufficient (typically: necessary but not sufficient without robust governance and adaptive management).

parts.content: Identify and explain two ethical issues associated with the harvesting of seals, whales, or dolphins
parts.markscheme:
### Animal Rights
- Whales, dolphins, and seals are intelligent animals, and harvesting them raises ethical concerns about cruelty. <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Indigenous Rights
- Harvesting may be culturally significant to indigenous communities, raising issues of cultural respect. <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Biodiversity Impact
- Reducing these populations may disrupt ecosystem balance, which some argue is unethical. <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Tourism Conflicts
- Some species are crucial for eco-tourism, leading to economic and ethical debates. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
**Table 1** displays various ecological characteristics measured at three different stages (A, B, and C) of a primary succession on a newly formed volcanic island.

**Table 1: Ecological characteristics of three stages in a primary succession**

| Characteristic | Stage A | Stage B | Stage C |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Species Diversity** | Low | High | Medium-High |
| **Total Biomass** | Low | Intermediate | High |
| **Mineral Cycling** | Open | Improving | Largely closed |
| **Net Productivity** | High | Decreasing | Low |

parts.content: Discuss the environmental risks of overexploitation in marine ecosystems.
parts.markscheme:
### Markscheme

- <marks>1 mark</marks> Biodiversity Loss: Overfishing reduces species diversity, impacting the food web.

- <marks>1 mark</marks> Habitat Destruction: Trawling and other practices can damage habitats, such as coral reefs.

- <marks>1 mark</marks> Species Collapse: Targeted fish populations can collapse, impacting predators.

- <marks>1 mark</marks> Bycatch: Non-target species, including endangered species, are often caught and discarded.

- <marks>1 mark</marks> Genetic Impact: Overfishing of certain sizes/ages can impact genetic diversity.

- <marks>1 mark</marks> Nutrient Cycling: Disrupts nutrient balance as fish populations decline.

parts.content: Distinguish between the perspectives of a **Cornucopian** and a **Deep Ecologist** regarding the management of global freshwater scarcity.
parts.markscheme:
Distinguish between the perspectives of a **Cornucopian** and a **Deep Ecologist** regarding the management of global freshwater scarcity.

- **Cornucopian perspective:**
  - Believes that technological innovation will always provide solutions to resource depletion (e.g., large-scale desalination/wastewater recycling)<marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Views the environment as a resource to be exploited for economic growth/human benefit  <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **Deep Ecologist perspective:**
  - Argues that nature/water has intrinsic value regardless of its utility to humans<marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Advocates for a radical reduction in human consumption/population to live within the natural limits of the ecosystem  <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Do not accept general definitions of EVSs that are not applied to the context of water scarcity. Comparison must be explicit or clearly implied to gain full marks.</note>

<marks>4 marks max</marks>

parts.content: Significant historical events have played a crucial role in shaping the modern environmental movement. Outline how **three** named historical influences have shifted global environmental value systems (EVSs).
parts.markscheme:
Significant historical events have played a crucial role in shaping the modern environmental movement. Outline how **three** named historical influences have shifted global environmental value systems (EVSs).

- **Silent Spring by Rachel Carson (1962):** Raised public awareness about the dangers of chemical pesticides (DDT) / highlighted the concept of bioaccumulation / catalyzed the ban of certain toxins <marks>2 marks</marks>
- **Bhopal Disaster (1984):** A gas leak in India that caused thousands of deaths / raised international concern regarding industrial safety, corporate responsibility, and the vulnerability of developing nations <marks>2 marks</marks>
- **Chernobyl Nuclear Disaster (1986):** A reactor meltdown in the USSR that released radioactive material across Europe / sparked a global debate on the safety of nuclear energy and the transboundary nature of pollution <marks>2 marks</marks>
- **Minamata Disaster (1950s):** Mercury poisoning in Japan caused by industrial waste / demonstrated the link between industrial pollution and human health/neurological damage  <marks>2 marks</marks>
- **Gaia Hypothesis (1970s):** James Lovelock’s idea that Earth is a self-regulating organism / encouraged a more holistic/ecocentric view of the planet <marks>2 marks</marks>

<note>Award 1 mark for the name of the event and 1 mark for its specific impact on the environmental movement/EVS. Max 7 marks including 1 mark for clearly articulating the general shift from apathy to concern.</note>

parts.content: To what extent do different environmental value systems (EVSs) influence the success of international agreements aimed at mitigating global plastic pollution?

parts.content: Explain the concept of "fish-in fish-out" (FIFO) ratio and its significance in sustainable aquaculture.
parts.markscheme:
- The "fish-in fish-out" (FIFO) ratio measures the amount of wild fish used to produce a given amount of farmed fish. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Lower FIFO ratios indicate a more efficient use of fish resources in aquaculture. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- The FIFO ratio is important for assessing the sustainability of aquaculture practices. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- High FIFO ratios can contribute to overfishing of wild fish stocks, as more wild fish are required for feed production. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Maximum 3 marks</note>

parts.content: Explain how the concept of maximum sustainable yield (MSY) can be used when planning sustainable terrestrial and aquatic practices in different types of food practices.
parts.markscheme:
The command term 'explain' means students must give a detailed account, including reasons or causes.

Consider any of the following marking points:

- MSY allows to calculate the greatest amount of harvest without impacting the needs for future generations. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- MSY is a measure of net (primary/secondary) productivity that can allow for sustainable planning and management of food production. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It involves measuring the annual increase in biomass for plants/yield of fish within a particular environment over a period of time. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This measurement allows farmers/fishers to estimate how much yield the area can extract while avoiding overexploitation. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Different food production systems will have different MSY depending on a number of factors:
  - Crop farming (e.g. soya beans) will have a higher sustainable yield when compared to cattle ranch farming due to a decrease in demand of additional resources such as land and water use. <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - The type of soil would affect overall yield as some soils are better suited for productivity, e.g., sandy soil in the Sahal desert will limit the overall yield of cash crops and over time this will continue to decline. <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Regenerative farming would need to carefully utilise the MSY findings over a longer period of time in order for the farm to become self-sufficient. Overpopulation of animals could clear vegetation leading to soil erosion and a decrease in food production. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In aquatic food production systems, the highest possible catch can also be measured to allow quotas to be set up for fishing. This can allow fishers/governments to advise on sustainable yield numbers so that future generations can also continue to benefit. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Ideally, it is recommended to stick to a yield below the MSY (optimum sustainable yield). This helps to factor in any other issues that could occur such as forest fires/droughts/diseases. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Link must be made to planning different types of food practices.</note>

parts.content: Explain the relationship between the second law of thermodynamics and the increase in entropy within an ecological pyramid of energy.
parts.markscheme:
### Second Law of Thermodynamics and Entropy
- The second law of thermodynamics states that the entropy (disorder) of a system increases over time <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In an ecological pyramid, energy is transformed from one trophic level to the next (e.g., from primary producers to herbivores)  <marks>1 mark</marks>
- These transformations are never $100\%$ efficient; a significant portion of energy is lost as low-quality heat to the surroundings <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This loss of heat represents an increase in entropy, meaning energy is degraded and becomes unavailable to do work for the next trophic level  <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Compare and contrast static equilibrium and steady-state equilibrium, providing a named example for each type.
parts.markscheme:
### Similarities
- Both represent states of balance within a system  <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In both states, the overall system properties (observable state) appear to remain constant over a period of time <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Differences
- **Steady-state equilibrium:** Involves constant inputs and outputs of energy and matter, but the system remains in a constant state (e.g., a mature forest ecosystem or a living organism)  <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Static equilibrium:** No inputs or outputs of matter or energy occur, and there is no change in the system over time (e.g., a pile of rocks or a chair) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Steady-state equilibrium is characteristic of open systems, whereas static equilibrium is generally associated with non-living/isolated components  <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Steady-state systems can return to equilibrium after a small disturbance through negative feedback, while a static system, once moved, establishes a new position  <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Examples
- Steady-state example: A pond (where water/nutrients enter and leave, but the pond remains the same)  <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Static example: A building or a geological formation (where no significant energy/matter throughput is required to maintain its structure)  <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award [4] max if no examples are provided or if only similarities/differences are discussed. For comparisons, ensure both sides are mentioned to earn the mark.</note>

parts.content: Discuss how positive feedback mechanisms can drive an ecosystem toward a tipping point, and evaluate the consequences using a named case study.
parts.markscheme:
### Content Guidance
Answers may include:

**Concepts of Feedback and Tipping Points:**
- Positive feedback loops are destabilizing; they amplify changes and drive a system away from its original equilibrium.
- A tipping point is a critical threshold where a small change can push a system into a completely new state/equilibrium.
- Once a tipping point is crossed, the change is often self-sustaining and difficult/impossible to reverse.

**Case Study: Arctic Ice-Albedo Effect:**
- **Mechanism:** Increased global temperatures melt sea ice $\implies$ darker ocean water is exposed $\implies$ albedo (reflectivity) decreases $\implies$ more solar radiation is absorbed $\implies$ temperatures rise further, melting more ice.
- **Consequences:** Loss of habitat for polar species (e.g., polar bears), alteration of global ocean currents (thermohaline circulation), and accelerated sea-level rise.

**Case Study: Eutrophication in a Lake:**
- **Mechanism:** Nutrient runoff leads to algal blooms $\implies$ algae die and decompose $\implies$ oxygen levels drop $\implies$ fish die $\implies$ more decomposition occurs, further stripping oxygen and releasing more nutrients from sediment.
- **Consequences:** Shift from a clear-water, biodiversity-rich state to a turbid, anaerobic, low-diversity state.

**Evaluation:**
- Discussion of the difficulty in predicting exact tipping points due to complex interactions.
- Analysis of the time lags involved in feedback loops.
- Consideration of the socio-economic impacts resulting from the shift to a new ecosystem state.

| Marks | Level descriptor |
|-------|-----------------|
| 0 | The response does not reach a standard described by the descriptors below. |
| 1–3 | Minimal evidence of understanding positive feedback or tipping points. Fragmented statements with little or no link to a specific case study. |
| 4–6 | Sound understanding of feedback mechanisms. Includes a relevant case study with clear identification of the feedback loop and the resulting shift in equilibrium. Some analysis of consequences provided. |
| 7–9 | Substantial understanding of the relationship between positive feedback and tipping points. Effective use of a detailed case study. Thorough evaluation of the ecological and/or social consequences. Explicit judgments regarding the stability and irreversibility of the system. |

questions.specification:
The following table provides data for two different species commonly produced in aquatic food systems.

| Parameter | Carp (_Cyprinidae_) | Bluefin Tuna (_Thunnus_) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Trophic Level | 2.1 | 4.8 |
| Primary Feed Source | Phytoplankton and detritus | Small pelagic fish (e.g., sardines, mackerel) |
| Feed Conversion Ratio (kg of feed to produce 1 kg of fish) | 1.4 : 1 | 15.0 : 1 |

parts.markscheme: <marks>1 mark</marks> for correctly identifying the point of maximum sustainable yield (MSY) on the graph.

parts.content: Explain the concept of MSY and its limitations in fisheries management.
parts.markscheme:
- MSY is the highest theoretical yield that can be continuously taken from a stock. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Assumes stable environmental conditions and population dynamics. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Does not account for ecosystem interactions or genetic diversity. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Can lead to overexploitation if used as the primary management target. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate the effectiveness of the three-level model of pollution management (Replace, Regulate, Restore) when applied to the issue of plastic waste in urban environments.
parts.markscheme:
### Understanding Concepts and Terminology
- Level 1: Altering human activity (Replace) - e.g., changing consumer behavior, bans on single-use plastics <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Level 2: Controlling release (Regulate) - e.g., legislation, recycling infrastructure, waste management taxes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Level 3: Clean-up and restoration (Restore) - e.g., ocean clean-ups, landfill remediation, removing microplastics from water <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sustainability and long-term vs. short-term solutions <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Breadth in Addressing and Linking
- Level 1 effectiveness: Most sustainable, addresses root causes, but requires significant cultural shift/political will <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Level 2 effectiveness: Practical for immediate reductions, generates revenue for infrastructure, but can be bypassed or poorly enforced <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Level 3 effectiveness: Necessary for legacy pollution, but often expensive, inefficient, and does not stop the source <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Examples
- Specific mentions such as plastic bag bans in specific countries, the Great Pacific Garbage Patch clean-up efforts, or municipal recycling schemes <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Balanced Analysis
- Discussion of how the most effective approach usually requires a combination of all three levels (e.g., Level 1 is hardest to implement but most effective; Level 3 is easiest to visualize but least effective long-term) <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Conclusion
- A clear judgment supported by the preceding arguments. 
- Example: 'While clean-up efforts (Level 3) are highly publicized, they are ultimately futile without robust Level 1 and 2 interventions to stop the influx of plastic into the system.' <marks>1 mark</marks>

<marks>9 marks max</marks>

parts.content: Explain how the physical and chemical properties of Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs) contribute to their biomagnification in arctic food webs.
parts.markscheme:
### Properties of POPs
- Chemical stability/persistence: POPs do not break down easily in the environment <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Solubility: They are lipid-soluble (fat-soluble) rather than water-soluble <marks>1 mark</marks>
- High molecular weight prevents easy excretion from the body <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Transport Mechanism
- Long-range atmospheric transport (the 'grasshopper effect'): POPs volatilize in warm regions and condense in colder, polar regions <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Biomagnification Process
- Bioaccumulation: Pollutants build up within the tissues of a single organism over its lifetime <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Biomagnification: Concentration of the pollutant increases as it moves up through successive trophic levels <marks>1 mark</marks>
$$
\text{Plankton} \rightarrow \text{Fish} \rightarrow \text{Seal} \rightarrow \text{Polar Bear (Highest concentration)}
$$

### Impacts and Examples
- Specific mention of a pollutant (e.g., DDT, PCBs, or Dioxins) and its impact on apex predators (e.g., thinning eggshells, immune suppression, or reproductive failure) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<marks>7 marks max</marks>

parts.content: Compare point source pollution and non-point source pollution, providing one specific example of each.
parts.markscheme:
### Point Source Pollution
- Definition: Pollution released from a single, clearly identifiable site or pipe <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Characteristic: Usually easier to monitor, regulate, and control legally as the culprit is known <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Example: A specific factory chimney emitting smoke or a sewage pipe discharging into a river <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Non-point Source Pollution
- Definition: Pollution released from numerous, widely dispersed origins (diffuse) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Characteristic: Difficult to attribute to one source; requires regional or international management strategies <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Example: Runoff of nitrates from agricultural fields or exhaust fumes from thousands of individual cars in a city <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 4 marks max. To achieve full marks, both types must be defined/characterized and both must have a valid example.</note>

<marks>4 marks max</marks>

parts.content: Based on the data in **Table 1**, outline four conclusions about the change in primary forest cover between 1980 and 2020.
parts.markscheme:
- Primary forest cover decreases overall from 12,500 $km^2$ (1980) to 4,100 $km^2$ (2020). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The largest absolute decline occurs between 1980 and 1990 (a loss of 2,700 $km^2$). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The rate of loss slows over time, with smaller absolute losses in later decades (e.g. 1,400 $km^2$ from 2010–2020). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- More than half of the 1980 primary forest cover has been lost by 2020 (loss of 8,400 $km^2$). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how biological characteristics can make species vulnerable to extinction, with reference to habitat fragmentation.
parts.markscheme:
- Small population size increases vulnerability to stochastic events (demographic/environmental) and local extinction in fragments. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduced gene flow between fragments increases inbreeding and lowers genetic diversity, reducing adaptive potential. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Narrow/specialized habitat or dietary requirements mean fragmented habitats may not provide sufficient resources/conditions. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Low reproductive rate/late maturity means slow recovery after population declines. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Limited dispersal ability or fixed migratory routes makes it difficult to move between fragments, increasing isolation. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Higher trophic-level species often require larger territories and stable prey bases, which fragmentation can disrupt. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Any other valid, explained characteristic linked to extinction risk under fragmentation. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Supported conclusion/value judgement about which characteristic(s) most strongly increase extinction risk in fragmented habitats (OWTTE). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss how different agricultural practices can influence the biodiversity of terrestrial ecosystems.
parts.markscheme:
**Markbands**

- **0 marks**: Not relevant or no standard reached.

- **1–3 marks**: Limited understanding of how agriculture affects biodiversity. Descriptive statements with weak linkage to biodiversity (e.g. monoculture “is bad”). Little or no balance between practices/impacts; few or no examples; conclusion absent or unsupported.

- **4–6 marks**: Some accurate explanation of multiple agricultural practices and their effects on biodiversity (e.g. monoculture vs polyculture; pesticide/herbicide use; fertilizer inputs; land clearing; grazing intensity). Some balance (benefits/trade-offs) and at least one relevant example. Conclusion present but may be limited in support.

- **7–9 marks**: Detailed, well-structured and balanced discussion comparing practices and mechanisms affecting biodiversity (habitat complexity, trophic interactions, edge effects, pollution, soil health, ecosystem services such as pollination). Uses appropriate ESS terminology and well-explained examples (e.g. agroforestry, IPM, organic vs conventional, rotational grazing, hedgerows/corridors). Explicit, well-supported conclusion/judgement on which practices best maintain biodiversity while meeting production needs (OWTTE).

parts.content: Discuss two risks associated with harvesting fish at maximum sustainable yield (MSY) levels.
parts.markscheme:
### Population Fluctuations
Environmental changes can cause fish populations to fluctuate, making MSY estimates inaccurate <marks>1 mark</marks>.

### Genetic Impact
Continuous harvesting may remove certain age groups, impacting genetic diversity <marks>1 mark</marks>.

### Ecosystem Disruption
MSY focuses on single species and may ignore ecosystem needs, affecting other species <marks>1 mark</marks>.

### Management Challenges
Accurate data is needed, and poor management can lead to overexploitation <marks>1 mark</marks>.

parts.content: Identify four strategies used in the sustainable management of wild fisheries.
parts.markscheme:
- Use of quotas / international regulations against overfishing/by-catch/fishing endangered species <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Designation of marine protected areas (exclusion zones) / restriction of fishing zones <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Restrictions/bans on types and size of fishing gear (including mesh size / bottom trawling / drift nets/ cyanide/dynamite fishing) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Restrictions on time/season/size/age allowed to fish <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Monitoring illegal fishing through technology/permits/licenses <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Research and support of calculation of maximum sustainable yield <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Labelling schemes to provide consumers with information on sustainability of fish <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Campaigns to reduce consumption of wild caught fish / promotion of aquaculture <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate the sustainability of two water management strategies to improve access to freshwater resources in a society.
parts.markscheme:
- Identifies an appropriate water management strategy (eg rainwater harvesting, desalination, dams/reservoirs, water transfers, groundwater abstraction, conservation/efficiency, wastewater treatment and reuse) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identifies a second appropriate water management strategy <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Gives one clear positive or negative sustainability evaluation point for strategy 1 (environmental/economic/social) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Gives a second clear positive or negative sustainability evaluation point for strategy 1 (environmental/economic/social) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Gives one clear positive or negative sustainability evaluation point for strategy 2 (environmental/economic/social) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Gives a second clear positive or negative sustainability evaluation point for strategy 2 (environmental/economic/social) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Supported conclusion/value judgement comparing the overall sustainability of the two strategies in context (eg depends on climate/energy mix/cost/equity; identifies which is more sustainable and why) <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Water Management Strategy #1: Rainwater Harvesting

Positive:
- Does not impact natural water cycle / replenishment rate / impossible to reduce natural income <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Free natural capital <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Useful for watering plants/irrigation / washing / fire protection / (thus) reduces consumption of groundwater/other freshwater resources <marks>1 mark</marks>

Negative:
- Availability restricted temporally and spatially (unequal distribution of rain / unpredictable supply) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Usually not safe for drinking <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Can't satisfy needs of irrigation of commercial agriculture <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Water Management Strategy #2: Desalination

Positive:
- Sustainable if energy required produced by photovoltaic cells <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sea water is more available than freshwater <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Provides accessible/safe drinking/irrigation water <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Salt may be used for producing useful chemical products (sodium hydroxide, hydrochloric acid) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduces pressure on freshwater reserves that need protection <marks>1 mark</marks>

Negative:
- Requires huge amounts of energy / increases Global Climate Change if fossil fuel used; not available in landlocked countries <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Building of facilities results in environmental damage/pollution <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Disposing of salt (brine) poses environmental hazards/pollutes ocean / salt is contaminated so can't be eaten <marks>1 mark</marks>
- High cost to build and operate that may be economically unsustainable <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each appropriately identified strategy for improving access to freshwater up to <marks>2 marks</marks> max.</note>

<note>Do not accept simplistic statements not clearly linked to sustainability, eg. cheap / efficient / doesn't need elaborate equipment/scientific expertise.</note>

<note>Award <marks>5 marks</marks> max if only positive OR only negative evaluation OR only one strategy evaluated. Award <marks>1 mark</marks> max for each clear negative or positive evaluation of each factor.</note>

parts.content: To what extent can different environmental value systems improve food production sustainability?
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

- Understanding concepts and terminology of environmental value systems; ecocentric/anthropocentric/technocentric; sustainability; sustainable development; environmental indicators; ecological footprint; natural capital & income; aquatic and terrestrial food production; soil; biodiversity; eutrophication;
- Breadth in addressing and linking inclusion of both aquatic and terrestrial systems; variety of systems in different climates; variety of factors affecting sustainability
- Examples of food production systems in terrestrial systems and aquatic systems;
- Balanced analysis includes ecocentric, anthropocentric and technocentric solutions and a mix of terrestrial and aquatic systems; and evaluation of their relative impact on sustainability;
- A conclusion consistent with, and supported by, analysis and examples given 

| Marks | Level descriptor |
|-------|-----------------|
| 0 | The response does not reach a standard described by the descriptors below and is not relevant to the question. |
| 1–3 | The response contains:• minimal evidence of knowledge and understanding of ESS issues or concepts • fragmented knowledge statements poorly linked to the context of the question • some appropriate use of ESS terminology • no examples where required, or examples with insufficient explanation/relevance • superficial analysis that amounts to no more than a list of facts/ideas • judgments/conclusions that are vague or not supported by evidence/argument. |
| 4–6 | The response contains: • some evidence of sound knowledge and understanding of ESS issues and concepts • knowledge statements effectively linked to the context of the question • largely appropriate use of ESS terminology • some use of relevant examples where required, but with limited explanation • clear analysis that shows a degree of balance • some clear judgments/conclusions, supported by limited evidence/arguments. |
| 7–9 | The response contains: • substantial evidence of sound knowledge and understanding of ESS issues and concepts • a wide breadth of knowledge statements effectively linked with each other, and to the context of the question • consistently appropriate and precise use of ESS terminology • effective use of pertinent, well-explained examples, where required, showing some originality • thorough, well-balanced, insightful analysis • explicit judgments/conclusions that are well-supported by evidence/arguments and that include some critical reflection. |
